rationale: The citing paper uses Nunes (2000) as core prior evidence that consumers can overestimate future usage and overpay in flat-fee versus pay-per-use decisions. It then leverages that account to derive a contrasting prediction and motivate its own tests, arguing that in multifunctional product choice consumers often fail to estimate usage at all. In the discussion, the authors explicitly position their contribution relative to Nunes by claiming they add a distinct usage-related mechanism beyond overestimation.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Nunes (2000)'s usage-estimation account and extends it to multifunctional product feature choice. It broadens the phenomenon from flat-fee service plans to feature-level product decisions and introduces a having-versus-using elaboration mechanism.
